content: The weather station is located in Jena, Germany, at approximately 50.9103° N, 11.5669° E, with an elevation of about 220 m above sea level.
Temperate oceanic climate, with warm summers, cool winters, and no pronounced dry season.
Air pressure generally exhibits a well-defined semi-diurnal cycle, featuring two daily maxima near 10:00 and 22:00 local time and two minima around 04:00 and 16:00.
Air temperature exhibits a diurnal cycle, typically decreasing to a minimum around 04:00-06:00 and rising to a maximum around 14:00-16:00 local time.
Potential temperature exhibits a diurnal cycle, typically decreasing to a minimum around 04:00-06:00 and rising to a maximum around 14:00-16:00 local time.
Tdew (degC) denotes dew point temperature. It reflects the moisture content of the air, with higher values corresponding to higher humidity and lower values to drier conditions.
Dew point temperature is higher and less variable in summer due to increased humidity, and lower in winter when the air is drier.
Dew point temperature closely tracks the air temperature under high humidity conditions, whereas it is substantially lower than the air temperature under low humidity conditions.

Dew point temperature exhibits a low-amplitude diurnal cycle, remaining relatively stable during the day and approaching the air temperature during the night and early morning, with variation influenced by humidity.
Relative humidity increases as air temperature falls at night and decreases as air temperature rises during the day.
Relative humidity tends to be higher during summer compared to winter.
VPmax (mbar) denotes saturation water vapor pressure.
Saturation water vapor pressure increases with rising temperature and decreases with falling temperature.
Saturation water vapor pressure exhibits a positive correlation with air temperature, attaining higher values during summer and lower values during winter.
VPact (mbar) denotes actual water vapor pressure.
Actual water vapor pressure generally rises or stabilizes at night and slightly declines or remains steady during the day.
VPdef (mbar) denotes water vapor pressure deficit, defined as the difference between saturation and actual water vapor pressure.
The water vapor pressure deficit increases with rising air temperature.
The water vapor pressure deficit decreases as relative humidity increases.
Specific humidity exhibits minimal diurnal variability.
Specific humidity shows pronounced seasonal variation, with substantially higher levels in summer and lower levels in winter.
Water vapor concentration exhibits pronounced seasonal variation, with higher values in summer and lower values in winter.
Air density decreases as air temperature rises during the day and increases as air temperature falls at night.
max. wv (m/s) denotes maximum wind velocity.
Precipitation amounts are higher from May to August.
Rainfall events occur more frequently from May to August.
Duration of precipitation is generally longer from May to August.
SWDR denotes short wave downward radiation.
Short wave downward radiation increases after sunrise, peaks around solar noon (approximately 12:00-14:00 local time), and declines to nearly zero after sunset.
Cloudy or rainy conditions significantly reduce short wave downward radiation.
Photosynthetically active radiation increases after sunrise, peaks around solar noon (approximately 12:00-14:00 local time), and declines to nearly zero after sunset.
Cloudy or rainy conditions markedly reduce photosynthetically active radiation compared to clear-sky days.
max. PAR denotes maximum photosynthetically active radiation.
Maximum photosynthetically active radiation increases after sunrise, peaks around solar noon (approximately 12:00-14:00 local time), and declines to nearly zero after sunset.
Cloudy or rainy conditions markedly reduce maximum photosynthetically active radiation compared to clear-sky days.
CO2 (ppm) denotes CO2-concentration of ambient air.
CO2 concentration of ambient air typically decreases during the day and increases at night.
CO2 levels are generally lower in spring and summer, and higher in autumn and winter.
